context: The NTSB investigated a surface incident at LAX where a fuel truck struck the left wing tip of a parked B787-9 at Gate 155 in Terminal B. The truck driver was operating without a wing walker as required by airport procedures and made a turn with insufficient clearance, striking the winglet at approximately 5 MPH. The impact caused a 3-foot crack in the winglet composite structure, requiring replacement at a cost exceeding $250,000. The aircraft was out of service for 4 days.
query: What procedural violation led to the aircraft damage at the gate?
expected_facts: fuel truck struck left wing tip at Gate 155
operating without required wing walker
3-foot crack in winglet composite structure
aircraft out of service for 4 days
negative_facts: the damage was to the right wing

context: While pushback from Gate B41 at DEN, our B737 MAX 9 was being pushed by a tug when the tow bar shear pin failed, disconnecting the tug from the aircraft. The aircraft was on a slight downslope and began rolling backward at approximately 2 knots. The ground crew set the aircraft brakes remotely using the interphone but the aircraft rolled an additional 15 feet before stopping. A baggage cart behind the aircraft was struck, causing minor damage to the cart and a small dent in the aft fuselage belly skin.
query: What mechanical failure initiated the pushback incident and what was damaged?
expected_facts: tow bar shear pin failed during pushback
aircraft rolled backward at approximately 2 knots
rolled additional 15 feet before stopping
baggage cart struck causing dent in aft fuselage belly skin
negative_facts: no contact was made with ground equipment

context: Taxiing our A321neo to RWY 8L at LAS via taxiway D, I observed a Southwest B737-700 taxiing in the opposite direction on the same taxiway. The taxiway width at that point was 75 feet. With our A321neo wingspan of 117 feet and the B737-700 wingspan of 112 feet, there was clearly insufficient clearance for simultaneous passage. I stopped and contacted ground control, who confirmed the other aircraft had been issued incorrect taxi instructions. The B737 was rerouted via taxiway E.
query: What geometry issue created the conflict on the taxiway?
expected_facts: opposing traffic on same taxiway D
taxiway width of 75 feet
A321neo wingspan of 117 feet
B737-700 issued incorrect taxi instructions
negative_facts: the conflict was on a runway

context: FAR 91.129(i) requires that each person operating an aircraft on the surface of a Class D airport must comply with any clearances, instructions, or procedures issued by ATC. AIM 4-3-18 states that pilots must read back all runway hold short instructions and not cross any runway hold position marking without explicit ATC clearance. Failure to comply with surface movement instructions has been identified as a leading cause of runway incursions.
query: What are the pilot responsibilities for surface operations at Class D airports?
expected_facts: must comply with ATC clearances and instructions at Class D airports
must read back all runway hold short instructions
must not cross hold position marking without ATC clearance
referenced in FAR 91.129(i) and AIM 4-3-18
negative_facts: Class D airports do not require radio communication
hold short readbacks are only recommended, not required
FAR 91.129 only applies to Class B airspace

context: The NTSB investigated a wing tip collision between two aircraft at MIA when a B747-400F taxiing on taxiway A made a tight turn onto taxiway P and its right wing tip swept over the hold short line, striking the vertical stabilizer of an ERJ-145 holding short of taxiway A. The B747-400F wingspan of 213 feet exceeded the available clearance for the turn radius. The ERJ-145 sustained damage to the vertical stabilizer requiring a ferry flight for repair. Contributing was the lack of wing tip clearance markings at the intersection.
query: What infrastructure deficiency contributed to the wing tip collision?
expected_facts: B747-400F wing tip swept over hold short line
struck vertical stabilizer of ERJ-145
B747-400F wingspan of 213 feet exceeded available clearance
lack of wing tip clearance markings at intersection
negative_facts: the collision involved two widebody aircraft
wing tip clearance markings were present

context: During low visibility taxi at SFO with RVR 800 feet, our B777-200ER was taxiing on taxiway C toward RWY 28R. We became disoriented and missed the turn at taxiway F, continuing straight toward the ILS critical area for RWY 28L. Ground control alerted us when we were 100 feet from the critical area boundary. We stopped immediately and were given vectors back to the correct route. An aircraft on approach to 28L had to execute a missed approach due to ILS signal interference concerns.
query: How did low visibility contribute to the surface navigation error?
expected_facts: RVR 800 feet caused disorientation
missed turn at taxiway F
approached ILS critical area for RWY 28L
aircraft on approach to 28L executed missed approach

context: While holding on taxiway N for RWY 4R at BOS, our Cessna Citation XLS observed a ground crew member walking across taxiway N without reflective vest or escort, approximately 300 feet ahead of our aircraft. We were at idle thrust and traveling at 5 knots. I stopped the aircraft and notified ground control. The individual was identified as a maintenance worker who had taken an unauthorized shortcut between hangars.
query: What unauthorized pedestrian activity posed a safety risk on the taxiway?
expected_facts: ground crew walking across taxiway without reflective vest or escort
worker was 300 feet ahead of aircraft
maintenance worker took unauthorized shortcut between hangars
aircraft was traveling at 5 knots
negative_facts: the worker was wearing a reflective vest

context: While holding short of RWY 17C at DFW on taxiway E, our A320 was cleared for takeoff. As we began our takeoff roll, tower instructed us to abort due to a Beechcraft King Air that had crossed the hold short line on taxiway WC at the departure end without clearance. We rejected the takeoff at 80 knots and came to a stop with approximately 6,000 feet of runway remaining. The King Air pilot stated he misidentified taxiway WC for taxiway WB.
query: What caused the runway incursion and how did the departing aircraft respond?
expected_facts: King Air crossed hold short line on taxiway WC without clearance
takeoff rejected at 80 knots
King Air pilot misidentified taxiway WC for WB
6,000 feet of runway remaining after stop
negative_facts: the incursion was by a jet aircraft
the takeoff was rejected at V1
the incursion occurred at the approach end

context: Our B737-800 was on final approach to RWY 8R at ATL when tower issued a go-around due to a Delta CRJ-900 that had crossed RWY 8R at taxiway E without hold short clearance. The CRJ-900 crew had been cleared to hold short of RWY 8R but misheard the clearance as 'cross RWY 8R.' We executed the go-around from 250 feet AGL. The CRJ-900 cleared the runway surface within 12 seconds of the tower alert.
query: What communication error led to the runway incursion?
expected_facts: CRJ-900 crossed RWY 8R without hold short clearance
crew misheard clearance as cross instead of hold short
go-around from 250 feet AGL
CRJ-900 cleared runway within 12 seconds
negative_facts: the CRJ-900 had been cleared to cross
the go-around was from 500 feet AGL

context: The NTSB investigated a serious runway incursion at JFK when a Delta A330-300 taxiing to RWY 4L crossed the active RWY 31L while a departing American B777-200 was at 1,000 feet in the takeoff roll. The A330 crew had incorrectly read back the taxi clearance, stating 'cross 31L' when the clearance was to 'hold short 31L.' The B777 crew observed the A330 on the runway and rotated early at approximately 145 knots. Minimum separation was estimated at 1,400 feet lateral.
query: What were the NTSB findings regarding the readback error and how close was the conflict?
expected_facts: A330 crew read back cross instead of hold short
B777 was at 1,000 feet in takeoff roll
B777 rotated early at approximately 145 knots
minimum separation estimated at 1,400 feet lateral
negative_facts: the readback was correct but the crew acted incorrectly

context: During taxi to the terminal at ORD after landing on RWY 28R, I inadvertently crossed RWY 14R at taxiway Y without a clearance. The ASDE-X system alerted the tower controller, who immediately issued traffic advisories. No aircraft were using RWY 14R at the time. I was operating a Pilatus PC-12 and was unfamiliar with the complex ORD taxiway layout. I had declined progressive taxi instructions from ground control prior to the incursion.
query: What pilot factors contributed to this runway incursion?
expected_facts: crossed RWY 14R without clearance
ASDE-X system alerted tower controller
pilot unfamiliar with ORD taxiway layout
declined progressive taxi instructions
negative_facts: the pilot had requested progressive taxi
the aircraft was a Cessna Citation

context: Our B787-9 was holding in position on RWY 25R at LAX awaiting takeoff clearance when tower cleared a Skywest EMB-175 to land on the same runway. The controller had forgotten our aircraft was on the runway. Tower caught the error 18 seconds later and issued a go-around to the EMB-175 at approximately 800 feet AGL. We were subsequently cleared for takeoff. The controller was placed on administrative leave pending review of the operational error.
query: What ATC error created the runway incursion situation?
expected_facts: controller forgot aircraft was holding in position on RWY 25R
cleared EMB-175 to land on same runway
error caught 18 seconds later
go-around issued at approximately 800 feet AGL
negative_facts: the pilot error caused the incursion
the controller caught the error immediately
the EMB-175 was cleared for a different runway

context: FAR 91.129(i) prohibits operating on or across any runway or taxiway at a controlled airport without authorization from ATC. AIM 4-3-20 defines runway incursion as any occurrence at an aerodrome involving the incorrect presence of an aircraft, vehicle, or person on the protected area of a surface designated for the landing and takeoff of aircraft. Runway incursions are categorized A through D, with Category A being the most severe involving a near collision requiring extreme action to avoid.
query: How does the AIM categorize runway incursions and what constitutes the most severe category?
expected_facts: runway incursions categorized A through D
Category A is most severe involving near collision
Category A requires extreme action to avoid
definition includes incorrect presence on protected surface area
negative_facts: Category D is the most severe
FAR 91.129 applies only to uncontrolled airports

context: The NTSB investigated a Category A runway incursion at DEN RWY 16R when a Frontier A321 commenced its takeoff roll while a United B737-900ER was crossing the runway at taxiway EE. The Frontier crew did not see the crossing traffic due to early morning sun glare. The B737 cleared the runway with an estimated 8 seconds to spare before the A321 reached the intersection at approximately 120 knots. The NTSB cited the tower controller's failure to ensure the runway was clear before issuing the takeoff clearance.
query: What factors combined to create this Category A incursion?
expected_facts: A321 began takeoff while B737-900ER crossing runway
Frontier crew did not see traffic due to sun glare
B737 cleared runway with 8 seconds to spare
tower controller failed to ensure runway clear before takeoff clearance
negative_facts: the Frontier crew saw the crossing traffic

context: While taxiing our CRJ-700 to the gate at IAD, ground control cleared us to cross RWY 19L at taxiway K. As we began crossing, a Cessna 172 on a practice ILS approach to RWY 19L was observed at approximately 1 mile final and 400 feet AGL. We expedited our crossing and cleared the runway hold short line with the Cessna approximately 3,500 feet from the threshold. Tower had failed to coordinate the runway crossing with the local controller handling the practice approach.
query: What coordination failure led to this runway incursion?
expected_facts: tower failed to coordinate crossing with local controller
Cessna 172 on practice ILS at 1 mile final
CRJ-700 cleared hold short with Cessna 3,500 feet from threshold
ground control had cleared the runway crossing
negative_facts: the Cessna was 5 miles from the threshold

context: At SFO during a land and hold short operation (LAHSO) on RWY 28R with a hold short point at taxiway F, our B737-800 was unable to stop before the hold short point due to a higher than expected touchdown speed. We crossed the LAHSO hold short line at approximately 30 knots while a Southwest B737 MAX 8 was on short final to RWY 1L, the intersecting runway. Tower issued a go-around to the SW aircraft at 200 feet AGL. The LAHSO distance available was 6,500 feet.
query: What operational factors led to the LAHSO violation?
expected_facts: unable to stop before LAHSO hold short point at taxiway F
crossed hold short line at approximately 30 knots
B737 MAX 8 on short final to intersecting RWY 1L
LAHSO distance available was 6,500 feet
negative_facts: the aircraft stopped before the hold short point
the intersecting traffic was on RWY 10L

context: FAR 91.113(b) establishes right-of-way rules for aircraft on the surface, stating that when two or more aircraft are approaching or taxiing on an airport for the purpose of departing, the aircraft to the other's right shall have the right-of-way. AIM 4-3-14 emphasizes the importance of maintaining situational awareness during surface operations and recommends using all available visual aids including airport diagrams, enhanced taxiway markings, and cockpit moving map displays to prevent runway incursions at unfamiliar airports.
query: What right-of-way rules and recommendations apply to surface operations?
expected_facts: aircraft to the right has right-of-way on the surface
situational awareness during surface operations emphasized
use of airport diagrams and moving map displays recommended
enhanced taxiway markings aid in preventing incursions
negative_facts: the aircraft to the left has right-of-way
FAR 91.113 only applies to airborne operations
AIM 4-3-14 discourages use of cockpit moving maps
